title: Working with Paths using path Module
description: Manipulate file paths safely with the `path` module.

slides.title: Why `path`? 🧭
slides.content:
# The Path Module

Working with file paths manually using string concatenation is error‑prone, especially across different operating systems (Windows uses `\`, POSIX uses `/`). The `path` module provides utilities to handle paths safely and consistently.

```javascript
const path = require('path');
```

slides.title: Common path Methods
slides.content:
## `path.join()`

Joins path segments using the platform-specific separator.

```javascript
const fullPath = path.join('users', 'alice', 'docs', 'file.txt');
console.log(fullPath); // users/alice/docs/file.txt (on POSIX)
```

## `path.resolve()`

Resolves a sequence of paths into an absolute path.

```javascript
const absPath = path.resolve('docs', 'file.txt');
// /current/working/dir/docs/file.txt
```

## `path.basename()`, `path.dirname()`, `path.extname()`

```javascript
const file = '/home/alice/docs/file.txt';
console.log(path.basename(file));     // file.txt
console.log(path.dirname(file));      // /home/alice/docs
console.log(path.extname(file));      // .txt
```

slides.title: Path Methods in Action

quizData.question: What does `path.extname('index.html')` return?
quizData.explanation: `extname` returns the extension including the dot, so '.html'.

slides.title: `__dirname` and `__filename` 📍
slides.content:
## Special Variables

In Node, every module has:
- `__dirname`: absolute path of the directory containing the current file
- `__filename`: absolute path of the current file

These are very useful when constructing paths relative to your script.

```javascript
const path = require('path');
const dataPath = path.join(__dirname, 'data', 'config.json');
```
